# Transcrição: 06-6.0 - Humor

- **Fonte:** 06-6.0 - Humor
- **Duração:** 14:53
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Alright, it's time to be funny. Now, humor is not required in a story, and I do tell stories where they're not funny in any way whatsoever. But humor is a tool, and it's powerful when deployed. When you make a person laugh, amazing things happen in their brain. Their brain actually releases three chemicals every time they laugh. And those three chemicals do amazing things that make them feel better and retain information better. And the brain associates the laughter with the thing that produced the laughter, which in this case would be you. That makes people like you more. Laughter can also be a wonderful thing in a story when the story is boring. Because sometimes parts of stories we have to explain things that are not really that exciting. So if we make them funny, they're less boring. We can also use laughter right before something terrible is about to happen to heighten the terribleness of the moment. And we can actually use laughter right after the terrible moment to give people a chance to take a breath to let them know that we just went through a really difficult time. Now let's all laugh and continue to move on with the story. Humor is very powerful. It's a really useful tool. I'm not telling you you have to be funny. But if you'd like to try to be funny in your story, I'm going to give you three strategies that I plan on using in the story I'm telling. I have more than 25 strategies that I typically teach. And I have products and ways of getting those strategies to you. But today we're going to deal with three. And I'm going to give you some hints on how to find humor in your story. Essentially the easiest place to find humor in your story is where you have the greatest optionality, which is to say it's the places in the story where you have the most choices in terms of what's coming out of your mouth. When you're repeating dialogue that someone has spoken, you can't be funny unless that dialogue happened to be funny. When you're in plot and you're describing what happened, it's really hard to be funny unless the things that are happening are inherently funny. But there are places and stories where there are greater moments of optionality and it's in those moments of optionality where you have the most choices that you can be funny. So one of the first strategies I'm going to teach you today is exaggeration. Now exaggeration is used in humor in such a way that the audience doesn't believe the exaggeration, but knows you're exaggerating for the sake of humor. In my story, I'm going to be describing the table that Clara is sitting at and eating her pancakes. It's a table inside of McDonald's Play Place, which is to say it's not the cleanest surface there ever was in the world. That is sort of the truth of the table. But what I'm going to say is something to the effect of it's a table laden with E. Coli or it's a table probably covered in flesh eating bacteria. That is an exaggeration, right? If it actually had E. Coli or flesh eating bacteria, my daughter would not be eating there. So I am going to

## [03:01 - 06:05]

exaggerate the condition of the table by heightening what could be wrong with it. If I really want to add a little extra layer of humor, I could also use specificity. I could go to the internet and look up the names of actual flesh eating bacteria. And if I find a really good name, the kind of name that really signals this bacteria will kill you if you touch it. If I find that perfect flesh eating bacteria name, that level of specificity will actually increase the humor even more. So that's a bonus strategy, specificity. The more specific we are, oftentimes the funnier we are. And the great thing about specificity is even if people don't laugh at your specificity, you're still just describing something. There are moments in stories when it's clear you were trying to be funny. And when the audience doesn't laugh, that hurts. It's like they've stabbed you in the back. But when you're using something like specificity, you're just describing things. And if it happens to make people laugh, great. And if it doesn't make people laugh, you just keep going because you are just identifying the specifics of that object or that scene or that person. So specificity is this wonderful thing that you can just sort of toss into the story with the hopes that it'll make people laugh. And if it doesn't, it's okay. So exaggeration, find moments in your story when you can heighten something to a certain degree. We exaggerate things like size, intensity, the degree at which we are feeling something or sensing something. We're looking for moments where we can just raise up the stakes or lower down the stakes in such a way that it's going to make people laugh. Scan your story. Look for places where you've described something. That's again, an area of optionality. Whenever we describe something when we're making choices about how we're going to describe it, the word choices indicates optionality. When you describe something, ask yourself, can I exaggerate it in such a way that it will make people laugh? The second strategy I want to give you is something I call definitions. Definitions is when we take something and we sort of reduce it to its basest form. We identify it through a definition, but we eliminate almost everything except for the one thing that will make it funny. I'm going to talk about the play place. Again, I'm going to lean in towards the disgusting nature of a McDonald's play place with all of these children sort of wandering around with snotty noses and bloody fingers and maple syrup all over their fingers. Like it's a gross place to take your child. And so what I'm going to describe it as something like this, I'm going to say I'm standing in a McDonald's play place, which amounts to an enormous Petri dish of disgustingness. I'll use something like a Petri dish. I'm going to define it in such a way. I'm going to eliminate the tubes and the fun. I'm going to take out the ball pit. I'm going to take out that fake grass on the floor. I'm going to take everything out of it and I'm going to pick the one thing about it that I can use to define it. I'm going to say play place equals Petri dish. And I'm going to find a way to sort of maybe exaggerate that Petri dish, maybe add some specificity

## [06:05 - 09:04]

to that Petri dish. I'm going to find a way to make it the funniest description possible, but I'm going to define it as a Petri dish. So when you're looking at your story, what you want to do is ask yourself, do I have objects? Do I have places? Do I have people who I can define with a singular characteristic, a singular, amusing characteristic? One thing that makes it funny and also at the same time continues to advance the story. By talking about the McDonald's play place as a Petri dish, I think I'm advancing the story. I'm giving you an accurate representation of the setting that we are trapped inside. And I think it's going to make you laugh at the same time. It's also going to make you laugh for a lot of other reasons. For example, when you find a definition that's sort of in contrast to what the thing actually is, that can be really funny. McDonald's play place is a fun place where children play. Petri dish is not a fun place where children play. And yet oddly, that definition works. When you can find a definition that is an opposition to what the object actually is or what the place actually is, that produces humor. I want you to go through your story and ask yourself, are there things I need to define within my story? Are there things I could define in my story? Are there objects or places or people who I need to explain? And if so, can I explain them with a definition, a singular attribute, a singular quality of that object or place or person that is both funny and still descriptive and therefore serves the story? I love definitions. I often think of it as this. I'm taking the idea of a play place and I'm throwing it into a colander. One of those things that you sort of rinse out your spaghetti. I throw play place into the colander and I sort of sift it out. I sift out everything from the play place except for the one thing left that is funny. So think about it that way. Take the object that you want people to laugh about or the person or the place that you want people to laugh about, throw it in your colander, sift out everything except for the one thing that's left that's funny and use that one thing to define it. It's an easy way to make people laugh and again, it's description, which is where optionality often hides. The third strategy I'm going to offer you today is prediction and I'm sure or almost sure you can use it in your story. Prediction is enormous optionality because it's essentially a moment in your story when you predict a false future and that false future is going to be amusing. It's often that moment where you have to make a choice in a story and there's the real choice you're going to make and then the multitude of other choices you could make. In that multitude of other choices that you could make is probably some funny options.

## [09:05 - 12:12]

For me, it's the moment that Charlie gets stuck in the tube. He's trapped up there and I don't know how to get him out. I have a multitude of options in front of me. Ultimately, Clara's going to become the option I choose and she'll agree to go save me. But what are other things I could have chosen? I could choose to call my wife, which is actually a thought I had. I'm going to have to call my wife and can you feel how this is going to be easy to be funny? I'm going to have to call my wife. I'm going to have to get her out of bed on a Sunday morning and tell her I have trapped her youngest child in a tube 12 feet over my head and I can't get him out. I am a grown ass man, a father, but I need the mother to come down to the play place to solve this problem for me. That's a prediction. It's not going to happen. But in my mind, I can tell my audience that that is a possibility and they will laugh at the possibility. And this is an enormous area of options because I can choose anything in the world to predict. I could say, I know I'll call the police because when your kid is stuck in a place and you can't get them out, you call the police. Baby Jessica was stuck in a well in the 1980s. What did they do? They called the police. This is sort of the reverse of baby Jessica. Instead of stuck in a well, he's stuck in a tube. I'm going to call the police and they're going to come down and they're probably going to arrest me for getting my child stuck in a tube or they'll laugh at me or the moment the police car pulls in, Charlie's going to see the police car and come right down. But anyway, this is not going to go well for me. Again, a prediction. Probably not going to use that one in the story. I feel like that one is a little too unbelievable. Our predictions should be plausible. They should be things that are realistic in the audience's mind. I don't know if realistically calling the police because my son won't come out of a tube is something that will land in the audience's mind is, yes, that is something I might do, but it would be funny. I could say I'm going to go get the manager. I'm going to get the manager of the McDonald's. This must have happened before. There must be stupid parents like me coming into this restaurant all the time. There must be terrible children like my son, Charlie, stuck in tubes all the time. They won't know what to do. I'm going to pull them away from their Egg McMuffins and their hash browns and their coffee. I'm going to tell them, stop working the drive-through because I have a small boy in a tube and I can't get him out. Could you please come save me? Because that's what you do when you have a problem. You turn to McDonald's employees and beg for them to help. That feels more plausible. I feel like I could make that funny. I'm probably just going to stick with my wife. I think one prediction is plenty in this story. I don't think I need another one, but you might find more than one. Sometimes making three predictions is just as good as making one, especially if they're small. What you want to do is, in your story, you want to find those moments where you had to make decisions, where there were tough choices, when you had a path to take, an A or a B. Can you throw in a C? I gave you three strategies. I actually gave you four

## [12:12 - 14:53]

if you were paying attention. We started with exaggeration, heightening the intensity, the degree, the size of something. I hinted at the power of specificity as well. Whenever you can be specific, it's probably going to be funnier. Then we talked about definitions, finding the places and the objects and the people in your story who you want to describe and perhaps you can describe with a singular amusing quality. Define them by one thing and make that one thing, the funny thing. Then I gave you predictions, predictions of the future, moments when you had to make a choice in your story, you had a tough decision to make. Before you tell us what the actual decision was, tell us a prediction of the future, the funny one, the one that is ridiculous or outrageous, but also plausible. Those are three of what amount to be about 25 strategies that I have for humor. The most important thing to remember about humor is this though. Humor tends to be predicated on surprise, meaning we assemble words in such a way that it causes the audience to be surprised by what we're saying. That is the essence of humor. I surprised you in such a way that it caused your brain to make you laugh. That is the basest colander version of humor. So when we're trying to be funny in stories, we're looking for moments of optionality and we're looking for moments when we can surprise our audience. Also remember this, you don't need to be funny. You might be telling the story about the death of your mother. I tell that story. And the story about the death of my mother, there's nothing funny at all. I don't make an audience laugh a single time because there's nothing funny about that story. So if you have a story that you don't think deserves humor or doesn't need humor or would be hurt by the inclusion of humor, don't be funny. Not every story needs to be funny. It is a tool in your toolbox and it is one you should use often when appropriate. In those moments where there's enormous optionality, in those moments when your story gets a little boring, in those moments when you want the audience to feel the passage of time, in those moments right before a terrible thing happens, so it feels a little more terrible. And in those moments right after something terrible happens to give the audience a chance to take a breath before you continue moving on. Those are all great times to use humor and many more to throw it in like a little bit of salt. Look at your story, find those moments that I've discussed where you can put humor in and see if it can work. All right, go ahead and do that now.
